title: Knot surgery and Heegaard Floer homology
speaker: Jennifer Hom
abstract: One way to construct new 3-manifolds is by surgery on a knot in the 3-sphere; that is, we remove a neighborhood of a knot, and reglue it in a different way. What 3-manifolds can be obtained in this manner? We provide obstructions using the Heegaard Floer homology package of Ozsvath and Szabo. This is joint work with Cagri Karakurt and Tye Lidman.

proof_stages.label: Dehn surgery and the first obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines p/q surgery on a knot in S^3, computes via Mayer-Vietoris that knot surgery has cyclic first homology, and uses the Lickorish-Wallace link-surgery theorem to frame the question of which manifolds are obtainable from a single knot.

proof_stages.label: Weight-one fundamental groups and remaining gap
proof_stages.summary: Using Seifert-van Kampen and the normal generation of knot-complement groups by a meridian, the speaker explains the weight-one fundamental-group obstruction and surveys known results showing why irreducible integer homology spheres remain the key target.

proof_stages.label: The Seifert-fibered family theorem
proof_stages.summary: For even p at least 8, the family Y_p=Σ(2,2p−1,2p+1) is stated to consist of integer homology spheres that are not surgery on a knot, are surgery on a two-component link, have weight-one fundamental group, and are rational-homology-cobordism distinct from each other and from Auckly's examples.

proof_stages.label: Heegaard Floer package and the d-invariant
proof_stages.summary: Heegaard Floer homology HF^+(Y) is introduced as a graded F[U]-module, its standard tower-plus-reduced form for integer homology spheres is described, and the d-invariant is defined as the lowest grading in the tower and recalled to be a rational homology cobordism invariant.

proof_stages.label: Floer-theoretic knot-surgery obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The key obstruction theorem is stated: if an integer homology sphere Y is surgery on a knot in S^3 and d(Y)≤−8, then U acts nontrivially on the grading-zero part of HF_red(Y).

proof_stages.label: Floer calculation for Y_p
proof_stages.summary: For the Seifert family Y_p, the needed Floer calculation is stated: d(Y_p)=−p and U acts trivially on the grading-zero reduced Floer homology.

proof_stages.label: Verification of the theorem clauses
proof_stages.summary: The non-knot-surgery claim follows by contradiction from the obstruction and the Y_p Floer calculation; the speaker then gives the two-component surgery description, proves weight-one from a Seifert-fibered group presentation, and separates the family by d-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Surgery numbers and open higher-component questions
proof_stages.summary: The result is reformulated in terms of integral and rational/Dehn surgery numbers, leading to the open question of whether some integer homology spheres require more than two link components.

examples.name: RP^3#RP^3 and the 3-torus
examples.note: These manifolds illustrate the cyclic-H_1 obstruction to being knot surgery, and are then revisited as link surgeries via an unlink and the Borromean rings.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes their first homology groups, notes they are not cyclic, and later describes link-surgery presentations for them.

passes.reason: These highly regular geometric manifolds are used to refute the claim that every closed 3-manifold is surgery on a knot by their noncyclic H1.

examples.name: Connected sum of three lens spaces
examples.note: The manifold L(p,1)#L(q,1)#L(r,1) with p,q,r relatively prime has cyclic H_1 but, by Howie's result, non-weight-one fundamental group, illustrating the strength of the pi_1 obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes the fundamental group as a free product of three cyclic groups, notes the homology is cyclic, and applies the weight-one obstruction.
